ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for dang gui roasted duck and roast pork noodle; mapped to broader noodle dish class FOODON_00002473.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for this Singaporean Chinese herbal noodle dish; A0FJP (noodle-based dish with meat) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for this dish; not mapped to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard hawker serving of Dang Gui Roasted Duck and Roast Pork Noodle is approximately 400 g, comprising egg noodles, sliced roasted duck, sliced roast pork, and a ladle of herbal duck broth or sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Dang Gui Roasted Duck & Roast Pork Noodle — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Dang Gui Roasted Duck & Roast Pork Noodle (cr_id G00010706). All per-100g values are taken as reported. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for roasted duck and roast pork composite; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: The HPB database records carbohydrate as 0g/100g for this entry, which reflects the meat-dominant analytical portion (roasted duck and roast pork without noodles). Because the carbohydrate content of the analysed portion is 0g/100g, glycemic load per serving of the analysed portion is 0. However, the full dish as consumed includes egg noodles; GI of 52 is assigned as an analogous estimate based on egg noodles (GI ~52, low category) per the University of Sydney GI Database. Consumers should note the full dish carries a meaningful glycemic load from the noodle component.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium at 1050 mg/100g, far exceeding the 600 mg/100g threshold, driven by dark soy sauce, oyster sauce, and seasoned duck broth.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, request minimal sauce and broth, and do not consume the soup base, to reduce sodium intake below 600 mg per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: The analysed portion records 0g carbohydrate reflecting the meat component only; the full dish with egg noodles carries a significant refined carbohydrate load that will raise postprandial blood glucose.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should request a smaller noodle portion or substitute with whole-grain or lower-GI noodles, and monitor total carbohydrate intake from the noodle component; the high sodium (1050 mg/100g) may also impair fluid balance and glucose management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1050 mg/100g) combined with cholesterol at 89 mg/100g, saturated fat from roasted duck skin and roast pork, and AGE formation from high-temperature roasting collectively elevate cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, remove duck and pork skin before eating, request less sauce to reduce sodium, and avoid consuming the broth to minimise combined sodium and saturated fat burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Roast pork (siu yuk) is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6) amplifying inflammatory cytokines; high-temperature roasting generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); sodium at 1050 mg/100g worsens joint swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid the pork component, remove duck skin to reduce AGE and saturated fat load, and request minimal sauce to limit sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dang Gui Roasted Duck and Roast Pork Noodle (当归烧鸭烧肉面) is a Singaporean Chinese hawker noodle dish combining two classic Cantonese roasted meats — char siu-style roast pork (siu yuk) and whole roasted duck — served over springy egg noodles dressed with dark soy sauce, oyster sauce, and lard or sesame oil. The defining characteristic is the addition of dang gui (当归, Angelica sinensis), a Chinese medicinal herb with a slightly bitter, earthy, and mildly sweet flavour that infuses the duck broth and roasting marinade, lending the dish a distinctive herbal depth. The duck is typically marinated with five-spice powder, soy sauce, and dang gui before roasting to achieve crispy skin and moist, flavourful meat. The roast pork contributes crackling skin and rich, fatty layers. The dish is served with a ladle of herbal duck broth and garnished with spring onions and sometimes blanched vegetables. Nutritionally, the HPB-analysed portion (meat and sauce component) provides 171 kcal/100g, 19.1g protein, 10.5g fat, and 0g carbohydrate, reflecting the meat-dominant analytical fraction; the full dish as consumed with noodles carries a significant carbohydrate load. Sodium is extremely high at 1050 mg/100g, making this dish a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. Cholesterol is 89 mg/100g. The dang gui herb provides some anti-inflammatory and circulatory benefit in traditional Chinese medicine but does not offset the high sodium and saturated fat concerns clinically. This dish is a popular lunch and dinner option at Singaporean hawker centres and coffee shops.
common_user_queries: What is dang gui roasted duck noodle?
Is dang gui duck noodle healthy?
How much sodium is in roasted duck noodle?
Can diabetics eat roasted duck noodle?
What is dang gui in Chinese cooking?
Is roasted duck noodle suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010706, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The entry is marked as canonical and reviewed. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central roasted duck and roast pork composites and noted accordingly.
notes: carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database for this entry; this reflects the meat-dominant analytical portion (roasted duck and roast pork with sauce, without noodles). The full dish as consumed with egg noodles will carry a significant carbohydrate load not captured in this per-100g figure. sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB for the analysed meat portion. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this entry and not available from a directly matching approved source. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this entry. saturated_fat_g estimated at 3.2g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central data for roasted duck (USDA FDC ID 171477) and roast pork composite; this is an approximation and should be verified against a direct HPB measurement when available. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on egg noodles per the University of Sydney GI Database; the HPB-analysed portion records 0g carbohydrate (meat only), so glycemic load of the analysed portion is 0. Dang gui (Angelica sinensis) is a traditional Chinese medicinal herb; its presence in the dish is noted for cultural and clinical context but does not materially alter the macronutrient profile.
